claims.claim_text: The Black Friday 2022 e-commerce outage story with exact VM counts, timings, revenue loss, machine families, and CEO quote.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: In December 2022, a fast-growing e-commerce company ran their annual holiday sale on Google Cloud. Their architecture was simple: a fleet of Compute Engine VMs behind an HTTP(S) load balancer, with a Cloud SQL database in the backend. At 9:01 AM on Black Friday, traffic spiked to 15x normal levels. The operations team had provisioned what they thought was enough capacity---24 VMs running `n1-standard-8` instances. Within minutes, all 24 VMs were at 100% CPU. The team scrambled to manually add more VMs, but each new VM took 3-4 minutes to boot, install dependencies, and register with the load balancer. By the time they had scaled to 60 VMs, they had lost an estimated $1.8 million in abandoned carts. The post-incident review revealed two failures: first, they were using individual VMs instead of Managed Instance Groups with autoscaling. Second, they were using an older machine family (`n1`) when the `n2` family offered 20% better price-performance. The CEO's summary was blunt: "We paid more for machines that were slower, and we could not scale them automatically."
claims.suggested_rewrite: Teams that run fixed pools of Compute Engine VMs without autoscaling can be overwhelmed by sudden traffic spikes, turning slow boot times and manual scaling into lost revenue.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed incident narrative with fake-precise operational and financial details, so it must be collapsed to the general lesson.

claims.claim_text: GKE nodes, Cloud SQL instances, and Dataflow workers all run on Compute Engine VMs under the hood.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: Compute Engine is the foundational compute service in GCP---even GKE nodes, Cloud SQL instances, and Dataflow workers run on Compute Engine VMs under the hood.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Compute Engine is a foundational GCP compute service, and understanding it helps you reason about how many Google Cloud workloads are executed.
claims.rationale: The exact cross-product architecture list is broader than one clean primary citation here and is not necessary for the teaching point.

claims.claim_text: GCP offers four machine families.
claims.span_hint: section: Machine Families, opening paragraph
claims.anchor_text: GCP offers four machine families, each optimized for different workload characteristics.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Compute Engine offers several machine families; this module focuses on four common categories for learning purposes.
claims.rationale: Compute Engine now has more than four machine-family categories, so the exact count is not stable.

claims.claim_text: The general-purpose section describes official E2, N2, and N1 series characteristics, including shared-core E2 options, N2 Intel platforms, and N1 legacy positioning.
claims.span_hint: section: General Purpose: The Workhorse, table
claims.rationale: Google Cloud's general-purpose machine-family documentation is the primary source for these series characteristics.

claims.claim_text: The N2D row claims 2 to 224 vCPUs and that N2D is often 10-15% cheaper than N2.
claims.span_hint: section: General Purpose: The Workhorse, N2D row
claims.anchor_text: | **N2D** | AMD EPYC | 1:4 (2 to 224 vCPUs) | Same as N2, prefer AMD | Often 10-15% cheaper than N2 |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **N2D** | AMD EPYC | 1:4 (series-specific limits) | General production workloads | Compare current N2D and N2 pricing in your region |
claims.rationale: The exact sizing and price-delta claim is not stable enough to leave as a fixed fact.

claims.claim_text: The T2D row claims best per-thread performance.
claims.span_hint: section: General Purpose: The Workhorse, T2D row
claims.anchor_text: | **T2D** | AMD EPYC | 1:4 (1 to 60 vCPUs) | Scale-out workloads | Best per-thread performance |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **T2D** | AMD EPYC | 1:4 | Scale-out workloads | Evaluate against current workload benchmarks |
claims.rationale: The superlative benchmark claim is load-bearing but lacks a stable benchmark source in the module.

claims.claim_text: Custom machine types use a universal vCPU-count rule of 1 or an even number between 2 and 224.
claims.span_hint: section: Custom Machine Types, rules list
claims.anchor_text: - vCPUs must be 1, or an even number between 2 and 224 (varies by series).
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Allowed vCPU counts depend on the machine series; check the current custom-machine-type limits for the series you selected.
claims.rationale: Custom-machine-type limits vary by series and no longer fit one universal numeric rule.

claims.claim_text: Custom machine types use a universal memory rule of 0.9-8 GB per vCPU, or up to 24 GB per vCPU with extended memory.
claims.span_hint: section: Custom Machine Types, rules list
claims.anchor_text: - Memory must be between 0.9 GB and 8 GB per vCPU (or up to 24 GB per vCPU with extended memory).
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Allowed memory ranges depend on the machine series, and extended-memory limits are defined per series rather than by one universal GB-per-vCPU rule.
claims.rationale: The current custom-machine-type rules are series-specific, and the module's universal memory limits are inaccurate.

claims.claim_text: The e2-micro row's relative-price figure of about 25% of e2-medium is an illustrative pricing claim.
claims.span_hint: section: Shared-Core Machines, e2-micro row
claims.anchor_text: | `e2-micro` | 0.25 shared | 1 GB | Micro-services, tiny APIs | ~25% of e2-medium |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | `e2-micro` | 0.25 shared | 1 GB | Micro-services, tiny APIs | Lower-cost than `e2-medium` |
claims.rationale: The relative percentage is region- and date-sensitive, but the general lesson that smaller shared-core machines are cheaper is valid.

claims.claim_text: The e2-small row's relative-price figure of about 50% of e2-medium is an illustrative pricing claim.
claims.span_hint: section: Shared-Core Machines, e2-small row
claims.anchor_text: | `e2-small` | 0.5 shared | 2 GB | Low-traffic web, dev | ~50% of e2-medium |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | `e2-small` | 0.5 shared | 2 GB | Low-traffic web, dev | Lower-cost than `e2-medium` |
claims.rationale: The exact percentage is illustrative and unstable, but the cheaper-than-e2-medium lesson is sound.

claims.claim_text: Spot VMs are the recommended successor to preemptible VMs; preemptible VMs have a 24-hour maximum runtime; preemption shutdown is best effort up to 30 seconds.
claims.span_hint: section: Preemptible and Spot VMs
claims.rationale: The preemptible VM documentation directly covers the recommendation to use Spot VMs, the 24-hour limit, and the preemption shutdown period.

claims.claim_text: Resource-based CUDs give about 28% off for a 1-year term.
claims.span_hint: section: Committed Use Discounts (CUDs), table row 1
claims.anchor_text: | **Resource-based** | 1 year | ~28% |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Resource-based** | 1 year | Varies by eligible resource and current pricing model |
claims.rationale: The exact discount depends on product and pricing model and is no longer safe as a fixed universal value.

claims.claim_text: Resource-based CUDs give about 52% off for a 3-year term.
claims.span_hint: section: Committed Use Discounts (CUDs), table row 2
claims.anchor_text: | **Resource-based** | 3 years | ~52% |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Resource-based** | 3 years | Varies by eligible resource and current pricing model |
claims.rationale: The exact 3-year discount is not a single stable universal number across current Compute Engine commitment models.

claims.claim_text: Spend-based CUDs give 25% off for a 1-year term.
claims.span_hint: section: Committed Use Discounts (CUDs), table row 3
claims.anchor_text: | **Spend-based** | 1 year | 25% (more flexible) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Spend-based** | 1 year | Varies by billing account model and eligible spend |
claims.rationale: The spend-based commitment program changed materially and this fixed number is not durable.

claims.claim_text: Spend-based CUDs give 52% off for a 3-year term.
claims.span_hint: section: Committed Use Discounts (CUDs), table row 4
claims.anchor_text: | **Spend-based** | 3 years | 52% |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Spend-based** | 3 years | Varies by billing account model and eligible spend |
claims.rationale: The exact 3-year spend-based discount is not a stable universal percentage in current documentation.

claims.claim_text: Sustained Use Discounts apply automatically after 25% of monthly use and effectively give about 20% off for VMs that run all month.
claims.span_hint: section: Committed Use Discounts (CUDs), SUD paragraph
claims.anchor_text: Sustained Use Discounts (SUDs) apply automatically---no commitment required. If a VM runs for more than 25% of the month, GCP automatically applies increasing discounts. By the end of the month, you effectively get a ~20% discount for VMs that ran the entire time.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Sustained Use Discounts (SUDs) apply automatically to eligible machine families---no commitment required. After 25% of monthly use, Google Cloud applies incremental discounts, and the maximum discount depends on the machine series and resource type.
claims.rationale: SUD eligibility and maximum discount percentages differ by machine family, so the module's universal statement is too broad.

claims.claim_text: Image families act as a latest pointer and default to the most recent active image in the family.
claims.span_hint: section: Image Families
claims.rationale: The custom-image deprecation documentation explicitly states that image families point to the most recent active image.

claims.claim_text: Instance templates are immutable and must be replaced with a new template to change settings.
claims.span_hint: section: Instance Templates
claims.rationale: The instance template documentation directly states that templates cannot be updated after creation.

claims.claim_text: Managed instance groups provide autoscaling, autohealing, regional multi-zone deployment, and automatic updating.
claims.span_hint: section: Managed Instance Groups (MIGs), opening paragraph
claims.rationale: The MIG overview page lists these core managed-instance-group capabilities.

claims.claim_text: Regional MIGs deploy instances across multiple zones in a region and improve availability versus zonal groups.
claims.span_hint: section: Managed Instance Groups (MIGs), regional guidance
claims.rationale: Google Cloud's MIG documentation explicitly states that regional MIGs span multiple zones and protect against zonal failure.

claims.claim_text: Google's global load balancing uses a single anycast IP and points of presence in more than 100 locations, terminating traffic near users and routing to the closest healthy backend with capacity.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 1
claims.rationale: The load-balancing locations documentation directly describes the single-IP anycast model, 100+ locations, and routing behavior.

claims.claim_text: The pd-standard row states 12 MB/s per GB throughput.
claims.span_hint: section: Disk Types and Storage, pd-standard row
claims.anchor_text: | **pd-standard** | 0.75 per GB | 12 MB/s per GB | Bulk storage, logs | Lowest |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **pd-standard** | 0.75 per GiB | 0.12 MiB/s per GiB | Bulk storage, logs | Lowest |
claims.rationale: The current Persistent Disk performance documentation uses per-GiB values that are two decimal places smaller than the module's throughput figure.

claims.claim_text: The pd-balanced row states 28 MB/s per GB throughput.
claims.span_hint: section: Disk Types and Storage, pd-balanced row
claims.anchor_text: | **pd-balanced** | 6 per GB | 28 MB/s per GB | General purpose (default) | Medium |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **pd-balanced** | 6 per GiB | 0.28 MiB/s per GiB | General purpose | Medium |
claims.rationale: The module's throughput figure is off by a factor of 100 relative to current Compute Engine documentation.

claims.claim_text: The pd-ssd row states 48 MB/s per GB throughput.
claims.span_hint: section: Disk Types and Storage, pd-ssd row
claims.anchor_text: | **pd-ssd** | 30 per GB | 48 MB/s per GB | Databases, high I/O | Higher |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **pd-ssd** | 30 per GiB | 0.48 MiB/s per GiB | Databases, high I/O | Higher |
claims.rationale: The current pd-ssd throughput limit is documented as 0.48 MiBps per GiB, not 48 MB/s per GB.

claims.claim_text: The local-ssd row states 900K IOPS and 9.4 GB/s total as if those were universal values.
claims.span_hint: section: Disk Types and Storage, local-ssd row
claims.anchor_text: | **local-ssd** | 900K total | 9.4 GB/s total | Temp storage, caches | Included with VM |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **local-ssd** | Varies by machine type and disk count | Varies by machine type and disk count | Temp storage, caches | Depends on the selected VM shape |
claims.rationale: Local SSD performance varies materially by machine type, interface, and the number of attached disks.

claims.claim_text: OS Login manages SSH access with IAM roles such as roles/compute.osLogin or roles/compute.osAdminLogin and disables metadata-based SSH keys when enabled.
claims.span_hint: section: Securing Access: OS Login and SSH Keys, paragraphs 1-2
claims.rationale: The OS Login setup documentation directly covers the required IAM roles and the disabling of metadata-based SSH keys.

claims.claim_text: Revoking IAM permissions instantly cuts off access to all VMs.
claims.span_hint: section: Securing Access: OS Login and SSH Keys, paragraph 3
claims.anchor_text: The moment their IAM permissions are revoked, their access to all VMs is instantly cut off.
claims.suggested_rewrite: When IAM access is removed, future OS Login SSH connections are denied across VMs that use OS Login.
claims.rationale: The exact timing word "instantly" is stronger than the primary docs support, but the broader access-control lesson is valid.

claims.claim_text: Instances without external IPs can be reached over SSH by tunneling through IAP TCP forwarding.
claims.span_hint: section: Securing Access: OS Login and SSH Keys, paragraph 3
claims.rationale: The IAP TCP forwarding documentation directly states that you can SSH to Linux instances without external IP addresses through IAP.

claims.claim_text: Spot VMs can provide discounts of up to 91% versus standard pricing.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 2 opening sentence
claims.rationale: The Spot VM documentation explicitly states discounts of up to 91% for many resources.

claims.claim_text: The exact n2-standard-16 on-demand and Spot prices and the derived $5,000 versus $500 monthly bill example.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 2 price example
claims.anchor_text: For a batch processing job running `n2-standard-16` instances, the difference between on-demand ($0.7769/hr) and Spot ($0.07-0.23/hr) can mean the difference between a $5,000 monthly bill and a $500 one.
claims.suggested_rewrite: For a batch processing job running `n2-standard-16` instances, the gap between on-demand and Spot pricing can materially reduce the monthly bill, but the exact savings depend on region, machine type, and current Spot prices.
claims.rationale: Exact VM prices are time- and region-sensitive and should not be frozen in teaching prose without a dated source.

claims.claim_text: Live migration during host maintenance typically causes much less than one second of disruption.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 3
claims.rationale: The live migration process documentation directly states that disruption is typically much less than one second.

claims.claim_text: Migrate is the default host maintenance behavior for most instances, while Spot VMs and preemptible VMs default to terminate behavior instead.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 3
claims.rationale: The host maintenance policy documentation is the primary source for default maintenance behavior.

claims.claim_text: The module gives an exact hourly price for m3-megamem-128 and a specific on-prem amortization comparison.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 4 sentence 2
claims.anchor_text: At full price, an `m3-megamem-128` costs over $21 per hour---which is still cheaper than buying equivalent on-premises hardware when you factor in a 3-year amortization.
claims.suggested_rewrite: These ultra-high-memory machines are expensive on an hourly basis, but they can still be attractive when you need short-term capacity without buying hardware.
claims.rationale: The exact hourly price is not stable, and the on-prem amortization comparison is an uncited opinion dressed as a cost fact.

further_reading.title: General-purpose machine family for Compute Engine
further_reading.why_relevant: Use this to verify current E2, N2, N2D, T2D, and N1 series characteristics and limits.

further_reading.title: Instance groups
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the primary overview for managed instance groups, including autoscaling, autohealing, and regional deployment.

further_reading.title: Application Load Balancer overview
further_reading.why_relevant: This gives the current product model for Google Cloud application load balancers and their global and regional modes.

notes: Mermaid diagrams, code blocks, quiz prompts, and hands-on solution steps were excluded unless a prose/table claim carried load-bearing vendor, pricing, or operational facts. The highest-risk problems in this module are the fabricated outage story, stale CUD/SUD pricing guidance, incorrect disk-throughput table values, and the wrong M3 sizing facts.
